bytes.topic: IaC Philosophy — reproducibility, drift detection, auditability, why IaC matters
bytes.title: Terraform's IaC Philosophy — Reproducibility, Drift, and Why State Is the Source of Truth
bytes.body:
Terraform's power isn't just provisioning — it's the contract between your declared intent and reality. Every `terraform apply` is idempotent: run it 100 times on the same config, you get the same infrastructure. This reproducibility is enforced through the state file (`terraform.tfstate`), which maps your HCL resources to real cloud objects.

Drift happens when someone manually changes a resource outside Terraform. Run `terraform plan` and you'll see it immediately — Terraform diffs the live API state against your state file and flags divergences. This is your audit trail: every `terraform apply` is logged, every change is reviewable in `tfplan` output or remote state history (Terraform Cloud/HCP Terraform).

Auditability is baked in via `-out=tfplan` + `terraform show -json tfplan` — you can parse exactly what will change before it happens. Pair this with `required_providers` version locking in `terraform.lock.hcl` and your infra becomes fully reproducible across teams and environments. IaC matters because undocumented clickops is a liability; Terraform makes every infrastructure decision a reviewable, versionable, reversible commit.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Detect drift without applying
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode
# Exit code 2 = drift detected

# Save and inspect a plan before applying
terraform plan -out=tfplan
terraform show -json tfplan | jq '.resource_changes[] | select(.change.actions != ["no-op"])'

# Lock provider versions for reproducibility
terraform providers lock \
  -platform=linux_amd64 \
  -platform=darwin_arm64

bytes.topic: Terraform Fundamentals — providers, resources, state, plan/apply lifecycle
bytes.title: Terraform's Core Loop — Providers, Resources, State, and Plan/Apply
bytes.body:
Terraform's execution model revolves around four primitives working in sequence. Providers are plugins that translate your HCL into API calls — `hashicorp/aws` v5 speaks AWS APIs, `hashicorp/azurerm` speaks Azure. Declare them in a `required_providers` block with pinned versions to avoid drift. Resources are the actual infrastructure objects: each `resource` block maps to a single API-managed entity, with a type (`aws_s3_bucket`) and a local name used for cross-references via expressions like `aws_s3_bucket.logs.id`.

State is Terraform's source of truth — a JSON snapshot in `terraform.tfstate` mapping your config to real-world resource IDs. Remote backends (S3 + DynamoDB for AWS, Azure Blob for Azure) enable team use and state locking. Never edit state manually; use `terraform state mv` or `terraform import` instead.

The plan/apply lifecycle is deterministic: `terraform init` downloads providers, `terraform plan` diffs desired vs. current state and emits a changeset, `terraform apply` executes it. Passing `-target=aws_instance.web` scopes apply to one resource — useful for debugging, but avoid it in CI pipelines as it can leave state partially applied.

bytes.topic: State Management — remote state, state locking, backend configuration
bytes.title: Terraform Remote State — Backends, Locking, and Why Local State Will Betray You
bytes.body:
Local `terraform.tfstate` is a single-developer convenience that breaks in teams. The moment two engineers run `apply` concurrently, state corruption is guaranteed. Remote backends solve both problems: centralized storage and state locking.

Azure Blob Storage, S3, and Terraform Cloud are the common choices. Every backend config lives in the `terraform` block — credentials are never hardcoded, they're injected via environment variables or partial backend configuration files passed with `-backend-config`.

State locking is automatic with supported backends (S3+DynamoDB, Azure Blob, GCS). When a plan or apply starts, Terraform writes a lock file. Any concurrent operation gets a `Error: Error acquiring the state lock` and exits cleanly — no silent corruption.

For multi-environment setups, use `terraform workspace` or, better, separate state files per environment via distinct backend `key` values. Never share state between prod and staging — a mistaken `destroy` in the wrong workspace costs you production. Use `terraform state list` and `terraform state mv` for surgical state manipulation without re-provisioning.

bytes.topic: Modules — reusable modules, registry, versioning strategy
bytes.title: Terraform Modules — Registry, Versioning, and Reuse Done Right
bytes.body:
Terraform modules are the primary unit of reuse: a directory with `.tf` files that accepts input variables and returns outputs. You can source them from the public Terraform Registry, private registries (Terraform Cloud/Enterprise), Git repos, or local paths. Registry modules follow a strict `<NAMESPACE>/<MODULE>/<PROVIDER>` naming convention and support semantic versioning via the `version` argument — something Git sources don't enforce natively.

Always pin registry modules to a specific minor version range like `~> 1.4` to allow patch updates while guarding against breaking changes. For internal modules, use a private registry or a dedicated Git tag strategy (`ref=v1.4.0`) — never point to a branch in production, as the ref is mutable. Structure monorepos with a `modules/` directory for reusable components and a `live/` or `envs/` directory for root modules that compose them. Pass outputs between modules using `module.<name>.<output>` references. Use `terraform get -update` to pull the latest pinned version after bumping the constraint.

bytes.topic: Workspaces & Environments — managing dev/staging/prod with the same codebase
bytes.title: Terraform Workspaces — Managing dev/staging/prod Without Duplicating Code
bytes.body:
Terraform workspaces let you maintain isolated state files for the same configuration. Run `terraform workspace new staging` and you get a separate `terraform.tfstate` for that environment — no separate directories, no code duplication. The current workspace is exposed via `terraform.workspace`, so you can branch resource sizing and naming inline.

The real power is combining workspaces with a `locals` map: define per-environment values keyed by workspace name, then reference `local.env_config` throughout. This keeps all environment differences in one block, not scattered across the codebase.

One hard rule: workspaces share the same backend bucket. Use key prefixes (`env:/<workspace>/terraform.tfstate` is the S3 default) and restrict prod workspace access via IAM policies or Terraform Cloud workspace permissions — never let a dev pipeline touch prod state. Workspaces shine for infrastructure that's structurally identical across environments. For environments with fundamentally different topology (e.g., prod has a multi-region setup dev lacks), separate root modules are the better call.

bytes.topic: Pulumi — real programming languages for IaC, type safety, testing support
bytes.title: Terraform vs Pulumi — Real Languages, Type Safety, and Actual Unit Tests
bytes.body: Terraform's HCL is purpose-built but hits a ceiling fast: no real loops without count/for_each hacks, no unit tests without Terratest spinning up real infra, and type errors surface only at plan/apply time. Pulumi replaces HCL with TypeScript, Python, or Go — the same languages you already test and lint. Your S3 bucket isn't a resource block, it's a typed object; passing a wrong argument is a compile error, not a failed apply 3 minutes in. Where Terraform forces you to work around dynamic blocks and locals for conditional logic, Pulumi lets you use native if/for. Testing is the real unlock: Pulumi's SDK ships a mock provider, so you can unit-test your stack logic without touching AWS at all — no state files, no credentials, no cleanup. You still get a state backend (Pulumi Cloud or S3-compatible), drift detection, and policy-as-code via CrossGuard. The migration path is incremental: pulumi convert --from terraform ingests your existing .tf files and outputs equivalent Pulumi TypeScript, letting you migrate module by module rather than all at once.

bytes.topic: Ansible — agentless config management, playbooks, idempotency
bytes.title: Terraform + Ansible — Bridging Provisioning and Configuration with null_resource
bytes.body:
Terraform provisions infrastructure; Ansible configures it. The standard bridge is a `null_resource` with a `local-exec` provisioner that fires an `ansible-playbook` command after `aws_instance` (or `azurerm_linux_virtual_machine`) creation. Pass the public IP directly from the resource output into the inventory argument — no static inventory files needed.

Ansible's idempotency model mirrors Terraform's: each module (`apt`, `service`, `template`) checks current state before acting. Re-running a playbook produces the same result, just like `terraform apply` on a converged stack is a no-op. This makes the pair safe to run in CI on every deploy.

The catch: Terraform provisioners only execute on resource creation, not every `apply`. To force re-execution when a playbook changes, key your `triggers` map on `filemd5("playbooks/web.yml")`. This gives you Terraform-native drift detection for configuration, not just infrastructure.

Never embed secrets in the `command` string — use `ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE` or pull values from `aws_ssm_parameter` data sources and export them as environment variables in the provisioner block.

bytes.topic: AWS CDK — constructs, stacks, L1/L2/L3 abstraction levels
bytes.title: Terraform to AWS CDK — Understanding L1/L2/L3 Construct Abstractions
bytes.body: Coming from Terraform, AWS CDK's three-tier abstraction model maps cleanly to concepts you already know. L1 constructs (prefixed `Cfn*`) are raw CloudFormation resources — equivalent to writing `aws_cloudformation_stack` in Terraform with hand-rolled YAML. No defaults, full control, maximum verbosity. L2 constructs are CDK's answer to a well-written Terraform module: opinionated wrappers that handle IAM roles, security group rules, and encryption flags automatically. `s3.Bucket` instead of `CfnBucket` — same resource, drastically fewer lines. L3 constructs (the `aws-solutions-constructs` library) bundle multiple L2s into repeatable patterns, like a curated Terraform registry module — `aws-apigateway-lambda` wires API Gateway + Lambda + CloudWatch in ~10 lines. CDK Stacks are the unit of deployment, analogous to a Terraform workspace: each stack synthesizes to a CloudFormation template with isolated state. Cross-stack references use `stack.exportValue()`, mirroring Terraform's `output` + `terraform_remote_state` pattern. The critical shift: CDK state lives in CloudFormation, not an S3 backend `.tfstate` file — `cdk deploy` is your `terraform apply`.

bytes.topic: Drift Detection — what happens when infra is changed manually, reconciliation
bytes.title: Terraform Drift Detection — Catching and Reconciling Out-of-Band Infra Changes
bytes.body:
Terraform drift occurs when real infrastructure diverges from your `.tfstate` file — typically from console changes, manual CLI commands, or out-of-band automation. Terraform's state is authoritative: if an EC2 instance type was bumped from `t3.medium` to `t3.large` via the AWS console, `terraform plan` will emit a diff proposing to revert it.

To detect drift without applying anything, use `terraform plan -refresh-only`. This syncs state from the live provider but stops before proposing resource mutations, letting you audit exactly what drifted. To legitimately adopt a manual change into IaC state, run `terraform apply -refresh-only`. To revert drift back to your declared config, a standard `terraform apply` is enough.

For automated drift detection in CI, use `-detailed-exitcode`: exit code `0` means no changes, `1` is an error, and `2` means drift was detected — treat that as a pipeline failure or alert trigger. Combine with `-out=drift.tfplan` and `terraform show -json drift.tfplan` to parse the `.resource_drift[]` array programmatically.

Always pin provider versions in `required_providers` — version skew between runs produces phantom drift from changed provider internals, not real infra mutations.
bytes.code_snippet:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

terraform init -input=false

# Refresh-only plan: detect drift, write plan file
terraform plan -refresh-only -detailed-exitcode -out=drift.tfplan
EXIT_CODE=$?

if [ "$EXIT_CODE" -eq 2 ]; then
  echo "Drift detected — diffed resources:"
  terraform show -json drift.tfplan | jq '.resource_drift[] | {addr: .action_reason, before: .before, after: .after}'
  exit 1  # Fail CI pipeline on drift
fi

echo "No drift detected."

bytes.topic: Testing IaC — terratest, checkov, tfsec, policy as code
bytes.title: Terraform Testing Stack — terratest, checkov, tfsec, and OPA in Practice
bytes.body:
Most Terraform bugs surface in production because engineers skip the testing layer entirely. A solid IaC pipeline has three distinct gates.

**Static analysis** runs first — `checkov` scans your `.tf` files for misconfigurations (e.g. S3 buckets with public ACLs, security groups with `0.0.0.0/0` ingress) before `terraform plan` even runs. `tfsec` overlaps but focuses on CVE-mapped security rules and emits SARIF output for GitHub Code Scanning.

**Policy as code** sits at the plan stage. Feed `terraform show -json tfplan.binary` into OPA's `conftest` to enforce org-wide rules — mandatory tags, approved AMI IDs, restricted instance types — without polluting modules with one-off `validation` blocks.

**Integration testing** with `terratest` spins up real infrastructure in an isolated AWS account, runs Go assertions against live resource attributes (`terraform.Output`, `aws.GetS3BucketPolicy`), then destroys everything with `defer terraform.Destroy()`. Gate this behind a nightly CI workflow — it's slow but catches drift that static tools miss entirely.

bytes.topic: Import Existing Resources — bringing existing infra under IaC management
bytes.title: Terraform import — Bringing Existing Infrastructure Under IaC Control
bytes.body:
Before Terraform 1.5, importing existing resources meant running `terraform import <address> <id>` to populate state, then manually writing the matching HCL — a tedious, error-prone process. Terraform 1.5+ introduces `import` blocks, letting you declare imports as code and use `terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf` to auto-generate the HCL skeleton.

Declare an `import` block with the target resource address and its provider-specific ID. On `terraform plan`, Terraform reads the live resource and emits a `.tf` file with all attributes populated. Review and clean up the generated config — remove computed fields Terraform manages automatically — then run `terraform apply` to finalize state.

For bulk imports, pair `import` blocks with `for_each`. Define a local map of IDs, reference it in both the `import` block and the resource block, and Terraform handles the entire batch in one apply. After import, always run `terraform plan` to confirm zero drift between your config and actual state before treating the resource as fully managed.

bytes.topic: Destroy Safety — preventing accidental destruction, lifecycle rules
bytes.title: Terraform Destroy Safety — Lifecycle Rules That Prevent Accidental Deletion
bytes.body:
Terraform's `terraform destroy` or a misconfigured `terraform apply` can obliterate production infrastructure in seconds. Three lifecycle meta-arguments are your primary defense.

`prevent_destroy = true` causes Terraform to hard-error if any plan includes destroying that resource — it won't even generate a destroy plan. Use it on stateful resources: RDS instances, S3 buckets, Kubernetes clusters.

`ignore_changes` prevents Terraform from treating external mutations as drift, stopping it from destroying and recreating resources that get modified outside IaC (e.g., auto-scaling adjustments, manual AMI updates).

For entire workspaces, combine `prevent_destroy` with a `-target` discipline: never run `terraform destroy` without `-target` in production. Add a CI guard using `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` and fail the pipeline if the plan contains any `destroy` actions against tagged critical resources.

In Terraform Cloud/Enterprise, use run policies (Sentinel or OPA) to block plans where `resource_changes[*].change.actions` contains `"delete"` on resources tagged `environment=production`. This shifts the safety left into policy-as-code rather than relying on human review.

bytes.topic: Cost Estimation — infracost, estimating before apply
bytes.title: Terraform Cost Estimation with Infracost — Know Your Bill Before `terraform apply`
bytes.body:
Infracost integrates directly into your Terraform workflow to surface cloud cost deltas before resources are provisioned. It parses your `.tfplan` output and maps resource configurations to real provider pricing APIs — no cloud credentials required for estimation.

Run `infracost breakdown --path .` in any Terraform module directory to get a full cost breakdown. For CI pipelines, use `infracost diff --path . --compare-to infracost-base.json` to show cost changes between the current plan and your baseline, making drift immediately visible in pull requests.

Key behaviors to know: Infracost reads `terraform.tfvars`, auto-detects modules, and supports workspace-aware pricing via `--terraform-workspace`. Resources without public pricing (e.g., private endpoints, some reserved capacities) show as `$0` with a warning — not silent gaps. Use `--show-skipped` to surface these explicitly.

For monorepos with multiple root modules, `infracost breakdown --path ./modules/networking --path ./modules/compute` aggregates costs across stacks in a single report, giving a unified pre-apply cost forecast.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Install Infracost
brew install infracost
infracost auth login

# Generate a plan file first
terraform plan -out=tfplan.binary
terraform show -json tfplan.binary > plan.json

# Run cost breakdown against the plan
infracost breakdown --path . --terraform-plan-flags "-var-file=prod.tfvars"

# In CI: diff against a saved baseline
infracost diff \
  --path . \
  --compare-to infracost-base.json \
  --format json \
  --out-file infracost-report.json

bytes.topic: CI/CD Integration — automated plan on PR, apply on merge, drift alerts
bytes.title: Terraform CI/CD — Automated plan on PR, apply on merge, drift alerts
bytes.body:
A robust Terraform CI/CD pipeline has three distinct gates. On pull request, run `terraform plan` and post the output as a PR comment using `github-script` — never merge blind. Use `-detailed-exitcode` to fail the check if there are pending changes, keeping plan output machine-readable. On merge to main, run `terraform apply -auto-approve` only after a successful plan artifact from the same commit SHA, preventing plan/apply drift between pipeline runs. Store state in a remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB or Terraform Cloud) with state locking so concurrent applies are blocked at the backend level, not just the CI layer.

For drift detection, schedule a nightly `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` job. Exit code `2` means real drift — pipe this into a Slack webhook or PagerDuty alert. Pair with `TF_CLI_ARGS_plan=-lock=false` so the scheduled read-only check never blocks active applies. Use workspaces or separate state files per environment to scope drift alerts precisely — you want "prod drifted", not "something drifted somewhere".
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/terraform.yml (abbreviated)
jobs:
  plan:
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    steps:
      - run: terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=tfplan 2>&1 | tee plan.txt
      - uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const plan = require('fs').readFileSync('plan.txt', 'utf8')
            github.rest.issues.createComment({ ...context.repo, issue_number: context.issue.number, body: '```\n' + plan + '\n```' })

  apply:
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    steps:
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan

  drift:
    if: github.event_name == 'schedule'
    steps:
      - run: terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -lock=false || curl -X POST $SLACK_WEBHOOK -d '{"text":"Terraform drift detected in prod"}'

bytes.topic: Linting & Formatting — tflint, terraform fmt, consistent style
bytes.title: Terraform Linting & Formatting — tflint, terraform fmt, and Enforcing Consistent Style
bytes.body: terraform fmt is non-negotiable in any Terraform workflow — it enforces canonical HCL formatting (2-space indents, aligned equals signs, sorted blocks) and is fully deterministic. Run it with -recursive to catch all .tf files in subdirectories. But fmt only handles style; it won't catch logic errors. That's where tflint comes in. tflint is a pluggable linter that catches provider-specific mistakes — like invalid aws_instance.instance_type values or deprecated argument usage — errors that terraform validate misses entirely. Install the AWS ruleset with tflint --init using a .tflint.hcl config file that declares the plugin. In CI, run fmt -check (exits non-zero without modifying files) before tflint, so formatting failures fail fast without masking lint output. Pair this with a pre-commit hook using the terraform-pre-commit hooks library to block unformatted or unlinted code at commit time. Together, these tools eliminate entire classes of plan-time surprises and keep multi-author modules readable without style debates.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Tool — Terraform vs Pulumi vs Bicep vs CDK decision framework
bytes.title: Terraform vs Pulumi vs Bicep vs CDK — A Terraform User's Decision Framework
bytes.body:
Terraform's state model and provider registry (3,700+ providers) make it the default for multi-cloud orgs — but the right tool depends on real constraints.

**Pick Terraform when:** You need multi-cloud in a single plan (`aws` + `azurerm` + `google` providers in one root module), your team lives in HCL, or you need Sentinel policy-as-code and drift detection via `terraform plan -refresh-only`. The `moved` block (1.1+) and `import` block (1.5+) let you refactor state without destroying resources — a migration superpower.

**Pick Pulumi when:** HCL's limited expressiveness blocks you — no first-class functions, no native unit testing. Pulumi's `Output<T>` and real language loops replace fragile `count`/`for_each` workarounds.

**Pick Bicep when:** You're Azure-only and want ARM's full API surface without JSON verbosity. No state file — Azure IS the state.

**Pick CDK when:** You're AWS-only and want L2/L3 constructs. Understand that it's CloudFormation underneath — you inherit the 200-resource stack limit and rollback behavior.

**The trap:** Never choose CDK or Bicep for multi-cloud. Never choose Terraform if your team won't write HCL — tool friction kills consistency faster than wrong syntax.
